question.question: Based on the detonation report, which of the following observed API calls or behavioral indicators provide supporting evidence for process injection activity in this infostealer sample?
options: A. RtlCreateUserThread@NTDLL.DLL — observed creating a remote thread in another process
B. GetDC — used to capture screen content from the desktop
C. WriteProcessMemory@KERNEL32.DLL — observed writing to a remote process's memory space
D. OpenProcess@KERNEL32.DLL — used to obtain a handle to a target process prior to injection
E. GetSystemTimeAsFileTime — used to retrieve the current system time for scheduling
F. NtResumeThread@NTDLL.DLL — used to resume a suspended thread, consistent with process hollowing or injection finalization
G. GetLocaleInfoW — used to query the system locale for language-based execution guardrails

question.question: According to the detonation report, which of the following API calls or behavioural indicators provide evidence supporting process injection activity by this sample?
options: A. WriteProcessMemory — the report records 'hash.exe' writing 32 bytes to a remote process
B. CreateToolhelp32Snapshot — called by 'hash.exe' to enumerate running processes prior to injection
C. IsWow64Process — called by 'hash.exe' to determine whether the target process is running under WOW64
D. RpcStringFreeA — indicates use of remote procedure calls for lateral movement
E. GetTokenInformation — used to query security token details for privilege assessment before injection
F. NtShutdownSystem — indicates the sample can reboot the system after completing its payload
G. EnumWindows — referenced in relation to ListPlanting (T1055.015), a window-based injection sub-technique

question.question: The detonation report contains several API calls and signatures associated with process injection activity. Which of the following observed indicators serve as supporting evidence for process injection behaviour in this sample?
options: A. CreateToolhelp32Snapshot — used to enumerate running processes prior to selecting an injection target
B. Process32FirstW — used to iterate over the process snapshot to identify candidate processes

D. GetVersionExA — used to retrieve OS version information for sandbox evasion decisions

F. DeleteService — used to remove security service entries to prevent detection of injected code

question.question: Examining the API call logs and signatures in the detonation report, which of the following pieces of evidence support the conclusion that hash.exe performs process injection into a remote process?
options: A. hash.exe called CreateToolhelp32Snapshot — used to enumerate running processes as a precursor to selecting an injection target
B. hash.exe called GetProcAddress — used to dynamically resolve addresses of injection-related APIs at runtime
C. hash.exe wrote 32 bytes to a remote process (T1055) — direct evidence of cross-process memory write
D. hash.exe called IsWow64Process — used to determine target process architecture before injecting shellcode or a DLL
E. hash.exe loaded the .NET runtime environment (CLR Loader DLL) — indicates managed code execution unrelated to injection
F. hash.exe creates a thread in process handle -1 — indicates thread creation within its own process space as part of injection setup
G. hash.exe opened the Kernel Security Device Driver (KsecDD) — used exclusively for cryptographic operations unrelated to injection

question.question: The detonation report for this infostealer sample contains several API calls and behavioural indicators related to process injection activity. Which of the following observed behaviours serve as evidence — either as direct injection primitives or as supporting reconnaissance activity — for process injection in this sample? (Select ALL that apply)
options: A. hash.exe wrote 32 bytes to a remote process (WriteProcessMemory-class behaviour, T1055)
B. microsofthelp.exe creates a thread in process (CreateThread/T1055.003 — Thread Execution Hijacking)
C. microsofthelp.exe called CreateToolhelp32Snapshot to enumerate running processes prior to injection targeting
D. hash.exe called IsWow64Process to determine process architecture as part of injection preparation
E. microsofthelp.exe called InternetOpenA to establish a network connection for C2 communication
F. hash.exe called FindFirstFileW to search directories for target files
G. Spawned process microsofthelp.exe identified as likely due to injection (T1055)

question.question: Based on the detonation report, which of the following behaviours specifically indicate that this ransomware is attempting to inhibit system recovery? (Select ALL that apply)
options: A. 'svchost.exe' disables Windows Task Manager via registry modification at HKCU\SOFTWARE\MICROSOFT\WINDOWS
B. 'vssadmin.exe' is executed with the command line 'vssadmin delete shadows /all /quiet' to remove volume shadow copies
C. 'bcdedit.exe' is executed with '/set {default} recoveryenabled no' to disable Windows Recovery
D. 'wbadmin.exe' is executed with 'wbadmin delete catalog -quiet' to destroy the backup catalog
E. 'hash.exe' opens files with deletion access rights targeting AppData paths
F. 'bcdedit.exe' is executed with '/set {default} bootstatuspolicy ignoreallfailures' to suppress boot failures

question.question: Based on the API calls and signatures observed in the detonation report, which of the following provide evidence supporting process injection activity by this sample?
options: A. CreateToolhelp32Snapshot — used to enumerate running processes as a precursor to selecting an injection target
B. AdjustTokenPrivileges — used to elevate token privileges required for cross-process memory operations
C. IsWow64Process — used to determine target process architecture before injecting shellcode or a DLL
D. LoadLibrary / GetProcAddress — used for dynamic API resolution to resolve injection-related functions at runtime
E. RtlGetVersion — used to retrieve the OS version as the primary mechanism for injecting into remote processes
F. CreateDirectoryA — used to stage a malicious DLL prior to injection via directory creation
